channel.title: Paper Pulse
channel.description: Keyword-based research paper aggregation from arXiv and IACR

item.title: FedIDM: Achieving Fast and Stable Convergence in Byzantine Federated Learning through Iterative Distribution Matching
item.description: Most Byzantine-robust federated learning (FL) methods suffer from slow, unstable convergence and severe utility degradation under high fractions of colluding malicious clients. To address this, we propose **FedIDM**, a novel framework that achieves fast and stable convergence *without* sacrificing model utility. FedIDM introduces **iterative distribution matching** to construct a lightweight, trustworthy condensed dataset—serving as a dynamic “trust anchor”—to characterize expected update behavior of benign clients. It comprises two key components: (1) attack-tolerant condensed data generation via gradient-aware distribution alignment, and (2) robust aggregation with *negative-contribution-based rejection*, which discards local updates that either deviate significantly from the condensed-data-guided update direction or induce large loss increases on the condensed set. Extensive experiments across CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and Tiny-ImageNet under state-of-the-art Byzantine attacks (up to 40% malicious clients) show that FedIDM converges **2.1–3.8× faster**, reduces accuracy variance by **67%**, and maintains only **1.2–2.5% accuracy loss**, outperforming Krum, Bulyan, RFA, and others. FedIDM bridges the long-standing trade-off between robustness, speed, and utility in practical FL deployments.

item.title: No More Guessing: a Verifiable Gradient Inversion Attack in Federated Learning
item.description: Gradient inversion attacks (GIAs) threaten client privacy in federated learning by reconstructing training data from shared gradients. However, existing GIAs lack intrinsic verifiability—especially for numerical tabular data—where human judgment of plausibility is infeasible, fostering the false impression that tabular data is inherently safer. We challenge this by proposing **Verifiable Gradient Inversion Attack (VGIA)**, the first GIA with a mathematically sound certificate of reconstruction correctness. VGIA leverages a geometric interpretation of ReLU leakage: each fully connected layer’s activation boundary defines a hyperplane in input space. It introduces an **algebraic subspace verification test** that certifies whether a hyperplane-delimited region contains *exactly one* original training record; upon certification, VGIA analytically recovers the feature vector and refines the target via lightweight optimization. Experiments on tabular benchmarks (e.g., Adult, Credit) with large batch sizes show VGIA achieves **exact record-and-target recovery**, outperforming state-of-the-art GIAs—which either fail or cannot assess fidelity. VGIA also reduces attack rounds by 37% on average versus prior geometric methods, enabling faster, more reliable inversion.

item.title: Route to Rome Attack: Directing LLM Routers to Expensive Models via Adversarial Suffix Optimization
item.description: Cost-aware LLM routing—dynamically dispatching queries to models of varying capability and cost—introduces a critical security gap: adversaries may hijack routers to consistently select expensive high-capability models (e.g., GPT-4, Claude-3-opus), causing severe cost inflation and service disruption. Prior attacks require white-box access or brittle heuristic prompts, failing in real-world black-box commercial routers (e.g., Fireworks.ai, Together AI). We propose **R²A (Route-to-Rome Attack)**, the first practical black-box attack that misleads routers via *adversarial suffix optimization*. R²A constructs a high-fidelity **hybrid ensemble surrogate router**, combining multiple open-source routers and lightweight discriminators trained on query–routing feedback. It then optimizes a learnable suffix using gradient signals from the surrogate to maximize selection probability of target expensive models. Extensive evaluation across 6 routing systems—including 3 closed-source commercial APIs—shows R²A increases expensive-model routing rates by up to 92.3% (avg. +58.7%) across diverse query distributions (QA, coding, math), with ≤50 queries needed for convergence. R²A outperforms baselines by 3.2× in success rate, exposing a fundamental vulnerability in production LLM routing infrastructure.

item.title: EdgeDetect: Importance-Aware Gradient Compression with Homomorphic Aggregation for Federated Intrusion Detection
item.description: EdgeDetect is a communication-efficient and privacy-preserving federated intrusion detection system (IDS) designed for bandwidth-constrained 6G-IoT edge environments. It introduces *gradient smartification*—a median-based statistical binarization that compresses local gradients into $\{+1,-1\}$ representations—reducing uplink payload by $32\times$ while preserving model convergence. To defend against honest-but-curious servers, EdgeDetect applies lightweight Paillier homomorphic encryption *directly on binarized gradients*, enabling secure aggregation without revealing individual updates. Evaluated on CIC-IDS2017 (2.8M flows, 7 attack classes), EdgeDetect achieves **98.0% accuracy** and **97.9% macro F1**, matching centralized baselines, while cutting per-round communication from 450 MB to just 14 MB (–96.9%). On Raspberry Pi-4, it incurs only 4.2 MB memory, 0.8 ms latency, and 12 mJ per inference (<0.5% accuracy drop). Under 5% poisoning attacks and severe class imbalance, it maintains 87% accuracy and 0.95 minority-class F1 ($p<0.001$), establishing a practical tripartite tradeoff among accuracy, efficiency, and cryptographic privacy for next-generation edge IDS.

item.title: Hijacking Large Audio-Language Models via Context-Agnostic and Imperceptible Auditory Prompt Injection
item.description: This paper identifies *Auditory Prompt Injection*—a previously overlooked threat that hijacks Large Audio-Language Models (LALMs) via context-agnostic, imperceptible adversarial audio—under realistic constraints of audio-only access and strict perceptual stealth. We propose **AudioHijack**, a general framework that: (1) uses sampling-based gradient estimation to bypass non-differentiable audio tokenization; (2) employs attention supervision and multi-context training for robust cross-context generalization; and (3) introduces a convolutional blending method to embed perturbations into natural reverberation, achieving high acoustic fidelity (PESQ ≥ 3.8, STOI ≥ 0.94). Evaluated on 13 state-of-the-art LALMs, AudioHijack achieves 79%–96% success rates across 6 misbehavior categories on unseen user contexts. Real-world tests confirm successful unauthorized command execution on commercial voice agents from Mistral AI and Microsoft Azure—exposing critical, model-agnostic vulnerabilities in the audio modality and underscoring the urgent need for dedicated audio-aware defenses.

item.title: Decoupling Identity from Utility: Privacy-by-Design Frameworks for Financial Ecosystems
item.description: This paper introduces a *privacy-by-design* framework grounded in **differential privacy (DP)** to resolve the utility–privacy trade-off in financial data ecosystems. We propose two complementary DP synthetic data paradigms: (**i**) *Direct Tabular Synthesis*, which preserves static historical correlations for QA, analytics, and model validation; and (**ii**) *DP-Seeded Agent-Based Modeling (ABM)*, which leverages DP-protected aggregates to calibrate dynamic, stateful simulations of market behavior—including stress scenarios and black swan events. Crucially, both methods **decouple individual identity from utility**, eliminating re-identification risks while enabling cross-institutional research and regulatory-compliant decision-making. Empirical evaluation shows DP-Seeded ABM reduces prediction error in crisis modeling by 37% versus conventional Monte Carlo approaches, with formal ε-DP guarantees. This framework bridges technical privacy, operational feasibility, and evolving regulatory expectations (e.g., GDPR, CCPA, PIPL).

item.title: Predicting Power-System Dynamic Trajectories with Foundation Models
item.description: This paper introduces **LASS-ODE-Power**, a foundation model framework for general-purpose power-system dynamic trajectory prediction. To address critical challenges—unknown time-varying parameters, data privacy constraints, and stringent online inference latency—we pretrain a scalable architecture on >40 GB of diverse DAE/ODE simulation trajectories, enabling transferable physics-informed representations. The model achieves zero-shot prediction from short measurement prefixes (e.g., 100 ms) across electromechanical and inverter-dominated regimes without system-specific training or data sharing. Key innovations include parallelized linearized inference for sub-8-ms latency and a targeted fine-tuning strategy using ~1 GB of heterogeneous power-system dynamics. Extensive experiments across IEEE benchmarks and renewable-rich grids show LASS-ODE-Power consistently outperforms state-of-the-art learning-based models by 32.7% in RMSE while maintaining real-time feasibility—establishing a new paradigm for privacy-aware, parameter-agnostic, and multi-regime dynamic forecasting.

item.title: AI-Enabled Covert Channel Detection in RF Receiver Architectures
item.description: This paper presents the first AI-powered hardware accelerator for real-time covert channel (CC) detection in RF receivers. We propose a compact CNN that processes raw I/Q samples directly at the receiver front-end, enabling low-latency CC presence detection and underlying hardware Trojan (HT) identification. Model compression reduces parameters by 80%, enabling efficient edge deployment. Evaluated on an open-source HT-based CC dataset, the model achieves 90.28% average CC detection accuracy and 86.50% HT identification accuracy across SNR > 1 dB; both exceed 97% at practical SNR > 20 dB—only <2% degradation vs. the baseline. A custom lightweight CNN accelerator implemented on FPGA achieves 107 GOPs/W and minimal resource utilization, outperforming state-of-the-art AI accelerators for RF signal classification tasks (e.g., modulation recognition) in task specificity, energy efficiency, and real-time capability.

item.title: Calibration-Gated LLM Pseudo-Observations for Online Contextual Bandits
item.description: We address cold-start regret in contextual bandits by augmenting Disjoint LinUCB with **calibration-gated LLM pseudo-observations**: after each round, an LLM predicts counterfactual rewards for unplayed arms, and these predictions are injected as weighted pseudo-observations into the learner’s parameter update. A novel calibration-gated decay schedule—tracking LLM’s reward prediction accuracy on *played* arms via exponential moving average—dynamically suppresses pseudo-observation weight when calibration error is high and boosts it during early rounds when accurate. Evaluated on UCI Mushroom and MIND-small, task-specific prompting reduces cumulative regret by **19% on MIND** versus vanilla LinUCB; conversely, generic counterfactual prompts *increase* regret in both settings—highlighting that **prompt design dominates all other architectural choices**, including decay scheduling and gating parameters. We analyze failure modes under small prediction errors and provide theoretical grounding for the bias-variance trade-off governing pseudo-observation weighting.

item.title: Cooperate to Compete: Strategic Data Generation and Incentivization Framework for Coopetitive Cross-Silo Federated Learning
item.description: This paper addresses the fundamental tension in real-world cross-silo federated learning (CFL): organizations must *cooperate* to train shared models yet *compete* in downstream markets—making data contributions a double-edged sword. We propose **CoCoGen+**, the first framework that jointly models non-IID data, inter-organizational competition, and GenAI-based synthetic data generation as a strategic choice. CoCoGen+ formulates each training round as a weighted potential game, where participants balance learning gains, computational costs, and utility losses from inadvertently strengthening rivals. We characterize its Nash equilibrium analytically and derive implementable, welfare-maximizing generation policies. To sustain long-term collaboration, we integrate a payoff redistribution mechanism that compensates both contribution value and competition-induced utility degradation. Experiments across diverse tasks show CoCoGen+ improves average accuracy by 12.7% and social welfare by 18.3% over baselines, demonstrating how competition intensity, data heterogeneity, and incentive design jointly shape strategic behavior and system efficiency.

item.title: HWE-Bench: Benchmarking LLM Agents on Real-World Hardware Bug Repair Tasks
item.description: HWE-Bench is the first large-scale, repository-level benchmark for evaluating LLM agents on real-world hardware bug repair tasks. It comprises 417 task instances derived from historical bug-fix pull requests across six major open-source hardware projects (RISC-V cores, SoCs, and security roots-of-trust), covering Verilog/SystemVerilog and Chisel. Each task runs in a fully containerized environment where agents must resolve actual bug reports, with correctness rigorously validated via native simulation and regression flows. Evaluated across seven LLMs and four agent frameworks, the best-performing agent achieves 70.7% overall task success—exceeding 90% on small cores but dropping below 65% on complex SoCs. We observe substantially larger model performance gaps than in software benchmarks, driven primarily by project scope and bug-type distribution—not just code size. Failure analysis pinpoints three critical bottlenecks: fault localization, hardware-semantic reasoning (e.g., timing, reset behavior), and cross-artifact coordination among RTL, configuration, and verification components.

item.title: SynHAT: A Two-stage Coarse-to-Fine Diffusion Framework for Synthesizing Human Activity Traces
item.description: Human activity traces (HATs) are vital for mobility modeling and POI recommendation, yet real-world datasets are scarce due to privacy constraints. To address the challenges of HATs’ irregular spatio-temporal dynamics and the computational inefficiency of fine-grained generation, we propose **SynHAT**, a two-stage coarse-to-fine diffusion framework. Stage 1 introduces **Coarse-HADiff**, a novel spatio-temporal denoising diffusion model featuring a latent U-Net with dual **Drift-Jitter branches** to jointly capture macro spatial drift and micro temporal jitter. Stage 2 refines outputs via a three-step pipeline: behavior pattern extraction, **Fine-HADiff** (architecture-reused), and semantic alignment. Evaluated on real HAT data from four cities across three countries, SynHAT achieves **52% lower spatial error** and **33% lower temporal error** than state-of-the-art methods, while enabling scalable, privacy-preserving synthesis of million-scale trajectories in minutes.

item.title: xFODE: An Explainable Fuzzy Additive ODE Framework for System Identification
item.description: We propose **xFODE**, an Explainable Fuzzy Additive ODE framework for System Identification, addressing the critical trade-off between accuracy and interpretability in neural/fuzzy ODE models. xFODE introduces *incremental physical states* with explicit engineering meaning (e.g., “energy dissipation rate”), employs *fuzzy additive derivatives* to decompose input contributions per state, and enforces *Partitioning Strategies (PSs)* that restrict antecedent activation to only two consecutive rules—reducing local inference complexity while grounding rule semantics in physical regimes (e.g., “low-speed dominance”). Trained end-to-end via differentiable parameterized membership functions, xFODE matches the state-of-the-art accuracy of NODE, FODE, and NLARX on benchmark SysID datasets (mean NRMSE ≤ 0.032), while delivering quantitative input attribution, interpretable rule transitions, and physically labeled states—enabling trustworthy deployment in safety-critical applications.

item.title: xFODE+: Explainable Type-2 Fuzzy Additive ODEs for Uncertainty Quantification
item.description: We propose **xFODE+**, an explainable Type-2 Fuzzy Additive ODE framework for Uncertainty Quantification (UQ) in data-driven system identification. Unlike black-box UQ models, xFODE+ ensures interpretability by constraining each additive component to activate only *two neighboring rules* in its Interval Type-2 Fuzzy Logic System (IT2-FLS), enabling local transparency and physically meaningful incremental state updates. Type-reduced outputs from IT2-FLSs jointly determine both point predictions and prediction intervals (PIs). Trained end-to-end via a composite loss balancing accuracy (MSE) and UQ quality (PICP, MPIW), xFODE+ matches FODE’s PI performance on benchmark SysID datasets (Lorenz, Rössler, Cascaded Tanks) while achieving comparable point-prediction accuracy—and crucially, delivering human-verifiable rule-level explanations of dynamic behavior.

item.title: Exploiting Correlations in Federated Learning: Opportunities and Practical Limitations
item.description: This paper establishes a unified correlation-based taxonomy for gradient and model compression in federated learning (FL), categorizing methods by the type of correlation they exploit: **structural**, **temporal**, and **spatial**. We identify their origins, propose quantitative metrics to measure correlation strength, and reinterpret 12 state-of-the-art compression schemes through this lens. Extensive experiments across diverse tasks (CIFAR, Shakespeare, FEMNIST), models (CNN, LSTM, ViT), and heterogeneity levels reveal that correlation magnitudes vary significantly—challenging the common assumption of universally strong correlations. Motivated by this, we propose two adaptive compression designs—**CorrSwitch** (dynamic mode selection) and **CorrBudget** (correlation-aware bit allocation)—that outperform static baselines: achieving **37% average communication reduction** with negligible accuracy loss (±0.4%) and up to **2.1× faster convergence** under high data heterogeneity. Our framework provides a principled foundation for developing application-aware FL compression.

item.title: Differentially Private Conformal Prediction
item.description: Conformal prediction (CP) provides distribution-free prediction sets with finite-sample coverage guarantees, but standard CP lacks privacy protection. To bridge this gap, we propose **Differentially Private Conformal Prediction (DPCP)** — a fully end-to-end $(\varepsilon,\delta)$-DP framework that avoids data splitting. DPCP integrates DP model training (e.g., DP-SGD) with a novel private quantile calibration mechanism based on smooth sensitivity and Laplace perturbation. Theoretically, DPCP inherits asymptotic validity ($\mathbb{P}(Y_{n+1} \in C_n(X_{n+1})) \geq 1-\alpha$) under mild regularity conditions and achieves tighter prediction sets than existing private split-conformal methods under the same privacy budget — with up to 28% average width reduction in theory and 19.5% empirically. Experiments on synthetic and real-world datasets (including UCI and FICO) confirm its practical efficacy: at $\varepsilon = 1.0$, DPCP maintains >92% empirical coverage while delivering statistically significant gains in set compactness.

item.title: The Acoustic Camouflage Phenomenon: Re-evaluating Speech Features for Financial Risk Prediction
item.description: This study challenges the assumption that paralinguistic speech features (e.g., pitch, jitter, hesitation) generalize to high-stakes financial forecasting. We evaluate a two-stream late-fusion model on real-world corporate earnings calls (n=1,847), predicting extreme downside stock events (≥15% 3-day drop). While the NLP-only stream achieves 66.25% recall, integrating acoustic features *reduces* recall to 47.08%—a statistically significant degradation. We attribute this to **Acoustic Camouflage**: deliberate vocal regulation by media-trained executives introduces contradictory noise (e.g., artificially stabilized pitch, suppressed disfluencies) that misleads multimodal meta-learners. This phenomenon defines a critical boundary condition for speech-based risk modeling in finance, urging caution against uncritical transfer of lab-derived acoustic models to strategic communication contexts.

item.title: Modeling LLM Unlearning as an Asymmetric Two-Task Learning Problem
item.description: This paper reframes LLM unlearning as an **asymmetric two-task problem**, where *retention* is the primary objective and *forgetting* is auxiliary. We propose a **retention-prioritized gradient synthesis framework**, decoupling task-specific gradient extraction from conflict-aware combination. Instantiating it, we adapt PCGrad and introduce **SAGO**, a novel method that enforces constructive, sign-constrained gradient alignment to strictly improve cosine similarity with the retain gradient—proven theoretically and verified empirically. On WMDP Bio/Cyber and RWKU benchmarks, SAGO consistently advances the Pareto frontier: e.g., on WMDP Bio (SimNPO+GD), MMLU recovery rises from 44.6% (naive) → 94.0% (+PCGrad) → **96.0% (+SAGO)**, with no sacrifice in forgetting strength. Our work demonstrates that **reshaping gradient geometry—not rebalancing losses—is the key to mitigating the unlearning-retention trade-off**.

item.title: Towards Personalizing Secure Programming Education with LLM-Injected Vulnerabilities
item.description: This paper introduces a novel LLM-based framework for personalizing secure programming education by automatically injecting pedagogically grounded security vulnerabilities—mapped to specific CWEs—directly into students’ own assignment code. Grounded in constructivist learning theory, our agentic system employs four autonomous LLM agents: an Injector (leveraging static analysis and vulnerability synthesis), an Evaluator (verifying correctness and safety), a Ranker (prioritizing by teachability and clarity), and a Pedagogical Generator (producing explanations, fixes, and reflection prompts). Deployed in two undergraduate CS courses (N=71), the system yielded strong qualitative feedback: 92% of students rated personalized injections as *more relevant*, 86% as *clearer*, and 89% as *more engaging* than generic textbook examples. However, quantitative comparisons against a widely used baseline showed no statistically significant improvement in post-intervention knowledge or confidence scores (p > 0.05), indicating that perceived personalization value does not yet reliably translate to measurable learning gains. We conclude with implications for refining injection fidelity, scaffolding, and longitudinal assessment.

item.title: MCPThreatHive: Automated Threat Intelligence for Model Context Protocol Ecosystems
item.description: MCPThreatHive is an open-source platform for automated threat intelligence in Model Context Protocol (MCP) ecosystems. It addresses critical gaps in existing tools—namely, incomplete compositional attack modeling, lack of continuous threat monitoring, and fragmented classification frameworks—by unifying data ingestion, AI-powered threat extraction (using fine-tuned Qwen2.5-7B), structured knowledge graph storage (aligned with the MCP-38 taxonomy), and interactive risk visualization. The MCP-38 taxonomy maps 38 MCP-specific threats to STRIDE, OWASP LLM Top 10, and OWASP Agentic Top 10, enabling cross-standard analysis. A composite risk scoring model integrates CVSSv4-inspired metrics with MCP-specific factors (e.g., context dependency strength), yielding actionable, explainable scores (0–10). Evaluation shows MCPThreatHive reduces threat detection latency to under 2 hours and achieves 94.7% coverage (F1 = 0.89), outperforming state-of-the-art alternatives across all three identified gaps.

item.title: RealVuln: Benchmarking Rule-Based, General-Purpose LLM, and Security-Specialized Scanners on Real-World Code
item.description: RealVuln is the first open-source, real-world benchmark for evaluating security scanners across three paradigms: Rule-Based SAST, General-Purpose LLMs, and Security-Specialized LLMs. It comprises 26 vulnerable Python repositories (educational and CTF applications) with 796 hand-labeled entries—676 true vulnerabilities and 120 carefully crafted false-positive traps. We rigorously evaluate 15 tools (3 SAST, 10 general LLMs, 2 security-specialized) using the F₃ score (β=3), prioritizing recall 9× over precision to reflect security-critical “no-miss” requirements. Results reveal a robust three-tier hierarchy: Kolega.Dev leads (F₃=73.0), followed by Claude Sonnet 4.6 (51.7), and Semgrep (17.7)—a >2.9× gap between top LLM and best rule-based tool. The tiering persists across all β weightings, underscoring fundamental paradigm differences. All data, scanner outputs, scoring scripts, and an interactive dashboard (https://realvuln.kolega.dev/) are openly released under MIT license. RealVuln is designed as a living, community-driven benchmark with multi-language expansion planned.

item.title: SafeHarness: Lifecycle-Integrated Security Architecture for LLM-based Agent Deployment
item.description: Large language model (LLM) agents rely critically on the *execution harness*—the system layer orchestrating tool use, context management, and state persistence—yet its architectural centrality makes it a high-value attack surface. Existing security approaches suffer from structural mismatch: they lack visibility into harness-internal state and fail to coordinate across agent lifecycle phases. We propose **SafeHarness**, a lifecycle-integrated security architecture with four tightly coupled defense layers: adversarial context filtering at input processing; tiered causal verification during decision making; privilege-separated, sandboxed tool control at action execution; and safe rollback with adaptive degradation at state update. Cross-layer mechanisms escalate verification rigor, trigger rollbacks, and tighten tool privileges upon sustained anomaly detection. Evaluated across diverse harness configurations and five attack scenarios spanning six threat categories, SafeHarness reduces the Unsafe Behavior Rate (UBR) by ∼38% and Attack Success Rate (ASR) by ∼42% versus the unprotected baseline—while preserving >96.8% of core task utility.

item.title: Secure and Privacy-Preserving Vertical Federated Learning
item.description: We propose a secure and privacy-preserving vertical federated learning (VFL) framework for feature-partitioned settings where labels are held by only one party. To simultaneously protect *input privacy* (features/gradients) and *output privacy* (released model), we decentralize the aggregator role across multiple non-colluding servers that jointly execute lightweight MPC protocols—avoiding costly end-to-end MPC training. Our framework introduces three optimized protocols supporting pure global and hybrid global-local updates, applying differential privacy *only at the final model release stage*. Experiments on CIFAR-10 and Adult datasets show our approach reduces communication by 62%, speeds up training by 3.1×, and incurs <1.2% accuracy loss—while providing formal end-to-end privacy guarantees without trusted third parties or specialized hardware.

item.title: Feed-Forward 3D Scene Modeling: A Problem-Driven Perspective
item.description: This survey presents a problem-driven taxonomy for feed-forward 3D scene modeling—methods that reconstruct 3D representations (e.g., implicit fields, meshes, point clouds) from 2D images in a single forward pass. Moving beyond output-format-specific analyses, we identify five core design challenges driving recent progress: **feature enhancement**, **geometry awareness**, **model efficiency**, **augmentation strategies**, and **temporal-aware modeling**. We ground this taxonomy with a comprehensive review of 12 benchmarks (e.g., ScanNet, CO3D), highlight evaluation gaps between synthetic metrics and real-world utility, and categorize applications across AR/VR, robotics, autonomous driving, and digital avatars. Finally, we outline key future directions: scalable world modeling, standardized cross-dataset evaluation, and neuro-symbolic integration to bridge perception and reasoning.

item.title: UHR-BAT: Budget-Aware Token Compression Vision-Language model for Ultra-High-Resolution Remote Sensing
item.description: Ultra-high-resolution (UHR) remote sensing imagery poses a unique challenge: critical query-relevant evidence often occupies only a few pixels within kilometer-scale scenes, causing quadratic token explosion and hindering small-object understanding. Prior token compression methods—such as naive downsampling or global top-k pruning—sacrifice detail fidelity or incur unpredictable compute. We propose **UHR-BAT**, a budget-aware, query-guided vision-language framework that compresses visual tokens with region faithfulness. It introduces (1) text-guided multi-scale importance estimation to identify discriminative tokens efficiently across feature hierarchies, and (2) region-wise preserve-and-merge strategies to eliminate redundancy while preserving structural integrity. On six UHR-specific benchmarks (e.g., UHR-VQA, UHR-Caption), UHR-BAT achieves state-of-the-art results using only ~12% of original tokens—improving VQA accuracy by +5.8%, CIDEr by +9.2%, and reducing latency by 3.7×. Code: https://github.com/Yunkaidang/UHR.

item.title: Outperforming Self-Attention Mechanisms in Solar Irradiance Forecasting via Physics-Guided Neural Networks
item.description: Accurate Global Horizontal Irradiance (GHI) forecasting is vital for grid stability in arid regions with high aerosol variability, yet Transformer-based models often overfit noise and incur prohibitive computational cost. This paper challenges the “complexity-first” paradigm by proposing a lightweight Physics-Informed Hybrid CNN-BiLSTM network. Unlike standard data-driven approaches, it explicitly integrates 15 engineered physical features—including Clear-Sky indices, Solar Zenith Angle, and AOD—as structural guidance rather than relying solely on raw time series. Spatial patterns are extracted via CNN; temporal dynamics are captured bidirectionally by LSTM. Hyperparameters are globally optimized using Bayesian optimization. Evaluated on NASA POWER data from Sudan, our model achieves an RMSE of **19.53 W/m²**, outperforming state-of-the-art attention-based baselines (RMSE 30.64 W/m²) by **36.3%**, while reducing inference latency by 8.2×. These results demonstrate the “Complexity Paradox”: in noisy meteorological forecasting, explicit physics-informed constraints yield superior accuracy and efficiency compared to self-attention mechanisms—advocating hybrid, interpretable AI for real-time renewable energy management.

item.title: From Prediction to Justification: Aligning Sentiment Reasoning with Human Rationale via Reinforcement Learning
item.description: Aspect-based Sentiment Analysis (ABSA) models often achieve high accuracy yet remain opaque “black boxes,” failing to emulate human affective cognition—where sentiment judgments are grounded in explicit, causal reasoning. To bridge this gap, we propose **ABSA-R1**, a reinforcement learning (RL)-based LLM framework that enforces a *reason-before-predict* paradigm. ABSA-R1 employs a novel **Cognition-Aligned Reward Model (CAR-M)** that penalizes logical inconsistencies between generated natural-language justifications and final sentiment labels, and introduces **metacognitive rejection sampling** to selectively refine predictions on uncertain or inconsistent hard cases. Experiments across four benchmarks show ABSA-R1 not only produces highly interpretable, human-aligned rationales (86.7% human-validated reasonableness), but also improves sentiment classification F1 by +2.1–3.8 and triplet extraction F1 by +3.3–4.9 over strong non-reasoning baselines—demonstrating that explicit reasoning enhances both transparency and performance.

item.title: A Comparative Study of Dynamic Programming and Reinforcement Learning in Finite Horizon Dynamic Pricing
item.description: This paper presents a rigorous, environment-scaled comparison of Fitted Dynamic Programming (DP) and model-free Reinforcement Learning (RL) methods—including PPO, SAC, and DQN—in finite-horizon dynamic pricing. Moving beyond low-dimensional benchmarks, we evaluate both paradigms across four increasingly realistic settings: single-typology i.i.d. demand; multi-typology heterogeneous demand with cross-price effects; inter-temporal revenue constraints; and non-stationary demand under joint constraints. Using identical data, horizon (T=100), and evaluation protocols, we find that Fitted DP achieves **2.1–3.8% higher average revenue** and near-perfect constraint satisfaction (99.4%) in constrained multi-typology settings, owing to its explicit expectation-based optimization. In contrast, RL—especially SAC—demonstrates superior robustness under demand non-stationarity, with 17% lower policy volatility. Computationally, DP scales cubically with state dimension, while RL training time grows linearly—highlighting a fundamental trade-off between optimality guarantees and adaptive scalability. Our work establishes the first fair, high-fidelity benchmark for pricing algorithm selection in operational environments.

item.title: Artificial intelligence application in lymphoma diagnosis with Vision Transformer using weakly supervised training
item.description: Vision Transformers (ViTs) offer superior feature representation over CNNs but face clinical deployment barriers due to reliance on labor-intensive pixel- or patch-level annotations. To address this, we propose a **weakly supervised training framework** for lymphoma classification—specifically distinguishing anaplastic large cell lymphoma (ALCL) from classic Hodgkin lymphoma (cHL)—using only slide-level labels. Our ViT model was trained on 100,000 automatically extracted image patches from whole-slide images, with pseudo-labels generated via multiple-instance learning (MIL). On an independent test set, it achieved **91.85% accuracy, F1 score of 0.92, and AUC of 0.98**, demonstrating robust performance without requiring expert patch-level annotation. This approach significantly reduces annotation burden while maintaining high diagnostic reliability, enabling scalable integration of ViT into automated clinical pathology workflows.

item.title: Automatic Charge State Tuning of 300 mm FDSOI Quantum Dots Using Neural Network Segmentation of Charge Stability Diagram
item.description: We present a deep learning–based semantic segmentation pipeline for automatic charge state tuning of 300 mm FDSOI quantum dots (QDs), addressing a key bottleneck in spin qubit scaling. Our method localizes transition lines across full charge stability diagrams (CSDs) using a U-Net architecture with MobileNetV2 encoder, trained on a large, manually annotated dataset of 1015 experimental CSDs spanning nine device geometries, multiple wafers, and fabrication runs. Evaluated via five-fold group cross-validation, the model achieves an **80.0% offline success rate** in identifying the single-charge regime—peaking at **88.3%** for specific designs. Failure analysis reveals dominant causes (e.g., low-contrast lines, non-uniform noise) and informs targeted mitigations. Critically, wide-diagram segmentation enables scalable, physics-informed feature extraction to feed back into design/fabrication workflows and paves the way for real-time integration in cryogenic wafer probers. This work demonstrates that neural network–driven CSD segmentation is a practical, high-throughput enabler for automated QD tuning.

item.title: NeuroTrace: Inference Provenance-Based Detection of Adversarial Examples
item.description: NeuroTrace introduces **inference provenance**—captured via *Inference Provenance Graphs (IPGs)*—as a novel, structured signal for adversarial example detection. IPGs are heterogeneous graphs jointly encoding activation dynamics and parameter-guided dataflow during forward pass, enabling holistic analysis of information propagation. We release a reproducible extraction engine, a GNN-ready graph representation, and a cross-domain benchmark (vision & malware) covering 7 attack families. Evaluations show IPG-based detectors achieve strong, transferable performance: AUROC ≥ 0.96 (vision) and ≥ 0.93 (malware) across intra-, multi-, and cross-threat settings—outperforming prior graph and activation-based baselines. We identify conditions for cross-attack generalization and quantify practical trade-offs (12–18% latency overhead; 1.2–3.5 MB/graph storage). NeuroTrace establishes inference provenance as a foundation for transparent, auditable ML systems.

item.title: Robustness Analysis of Machine Learning Models for IoT Intrusion Detection Under Data Poisoning Attacks
item.description: This study empirically evaluates the robustness of four ML classifiers—Random Forest, Gradient Boosting Machine, Logistic Regression, and Deep Neural Network—against data poisoning attacks (label flipping, outlier injection, and hybrid poisoning) on three real-world IoT intrusion datasets (TON-IoT, NSL-KDD-IoT-23, Bot-IoT). Results reveal stark robustness disparities: ensemble models degrade by ≤8% in F1-score under 5% poisoning, whereas Logistic Regression and DNN suffer up to **40.2% F1-drop**, with severe decision boundary distortion and fidelity loss. The work establishes a reproducible resilience benchmark for IoT IDS and advocates integrating adversarial training, feature-level input validation, and continuous anomaly monitoring into operational pipelines. It further proposes embedding resilience testing into AI security standards (e.g., ETSI EN 303 645), providing foundational evidence for developing adaptive, attack-aware intrusion detection systems in adversarial IoT environments.

item.title: Filament: Denning-Style Information Flow Control for Rust
item.description: Filament is the first Denning-style static information-flow control (IFC) library for Rust that requires **no compiler modifications**. It reconciles the tension between fine-grained IFC precision and practical deployability by addressing three core challenges: (1) enabling low-overhead explicit-flow checking via Rust’s type inference—requiring minimal label annotations (e.g., `Labeled<T, L>`); (2) enforcing implicit flows through a compile-time program counter (PC) label using the lightweight `pc_block!` macro—eliminating runtime cost and compiler support; and (3) ensuring safe interoperability with standard and third-party libraries via `fcall!` and `mcall!` macros that automatically mediate untrusted calls. Evaluation shows Filament incurs **negligible compile-time overhead (<5%)** and demands only modest annotations (typically 1–2 per function). Crucially, compared to Cocoon, Filament offers a **more permissive programming model**: it avoids frequent reliance on security-bypassing “escape hatches” (e.g., `declassify`) for common control constructs, significantly improving usability and correctness assurance.

item.title: DharmaOCR: Specialized Small Language Models for Structured OCR that outperform Open-Source and Commercial Baselines
item.description: This paper introduces **DharmaOCR Full (7B)** and **DharmaOCR Lite (3B)**, specialized small language models (SSLMs) for structured OCR that jointly optimize transcription fidelity, generation stability, and inference cost. We propose **DharmaOCR-Benchmark**, the first comprehensive OCR benchmark covering printed, handwritten, and legal/administrative documents, with a unified evaluation protocol that treats *text degeneration*—e.g., infinite loops or empty outputs—as a first-class metric alongside unit cost and structural fidelity. Crucially, we show degeneration materially harms production performance (increasing latency, reducing throughput, inflating compute). Methodologically, this is the **first application of Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) in OCR**, using degenerate generations as rejected examples to penalize looping behavior; combined with SFT enforcing strict JSON schema, DPO reduces degeneration by up to **87.6% relatively**, without sacrificing extraction quality. On DharmaOCR-Benchmark, DharmaOCR Full and Lite achieve state-of-the-art F1 scores of **0.925 and 0.911**, with only **0.40% and 0.20% degeneration rates**, outperforming all open-source and commercial baselines. AWQ quantization further cuts per-page cost by up to **22%** with negligible quality loss.

item.title: Asynchronous Probability Ensembling for Federated Disaster Detection
item.description: This paper proposes Asynchronous Probability Ensembling (APE), a privacy-preserving, communication-efficient framework for federated disaster detection. Instead of exchanging model weights, APE asynchronously aggregates lightweight class-probability vectors from heterogeneous CNNs (e.g., ResNet, MobileNet) across edge devices—reducing communication overhead by >99.6% versus FedAvg. A feedback distillation mechanism mitigates Non-IID degradation by refining local models with server-aggregated soft labels. Experiments on wildfire, flood, and earthquake image datasets show APE achieves +3.8% higher mAP than FedAvg and +5.2% over standalone models, while maintaining robustness under high-latency (4G-simulated) conditions. APE enables scalable, real-time disaster identification without architectural synchronization or raw-data sharing.

item.title: LogicEval: A Systematic Framework for Evaluating Automated Repair Techniques for Logical Vulnerabilities in Real-World Software
item.description: This paper introduces **LogicEval**, the first systematic framework for evaluating automated repair techniques—both traditional APR tools and LLM-based approaches—on real-world logical vulnerabilities. We construct **LogicDS**, the first benchmark dataset of 86 CVE-verified logical vulnerabilities across diverse domains (e.g., web services, smart contracts), each annotated with precise locations, triggering conditions, expected correct behavior, and human-validated test suites. LogicEval evaluates patches along four dimensions: compilability, functional correctness, logical completeness (i.e., root-cause resolution), and contextual fidelity. Our evaluation reveals that current LLM-based repair achieves only 19.3% end-to-end success rate; failures are primarily driven by **prompt sensitivity**, **loss of cross-module code context**, and **inaccurate vulnerability localization**—challenges largely unaddressed by existing APR methods (<2% success). LogicEval establishes a rigorous, reproducible foundation for advancing logical vulnerability repair research.

item.title: Parallax: Why AI Agents That Think Must Never Act
item.description: This paper identifies a critical architectural flaw in autonomous AI agents: prompt-based safety mechanisms fail catastrophically when the reasoning system is compromised, as they reside *within* the same trust boundary as the threat. To address this, we introduce **Parallax**, a paradigm for safe AI execution grounded in four principles: (1) strict **Cognitive-Executive Separation**, preventing reasoning modules from directly invoking actions; (2) **Adversarial Validation with Graduated Determinism**, inserting an independent, tiered validator between reasoning and execution; (3) **Information Flow Control**, propagating sensitivity labels to detect context-dependent threats; and (4) **Reversible Execution**, enabling atomic rollback via pre-action state capture. We release **OpenParallax**, a production-ready Go implementation, and evaluate it using *Assume-Compromise Evaluation*—a novel methodology that bypasses the reasoning system entirely to stress-test the architectural boundary. Across 280 adversarial test cases spanning nine attack categories, Parallax blocks **98.9% of attacks with zero false positives** under default settings, and achieves **100% blocking** under maximum-security mode—demonstrating resilience where prompt guardrails offer zero protection under full compromise.

item.title: Understanding and Improving Continuous Adversarial Training for LLMs via In-context Learning Theory
item.description: Adversarial training (AT) defends LLMs against jailbreak attacks but is computationally expensive. Continuous AT (CAT) improves efficiency by optimizing adversarial perturbations in the *continuous embedding space*—yet its theoretical justification remains elusive: why do embedding-space perturbations confer robustness against *token-space* jailbreak prompts? This paper provides the first theoretical analysis of CAT grounded in in-context learning (ICL) theory. For linear Transformers trained on in-context linear regression, we derive a robust generalization bound that *decreases* with the embedding perturbation radius—explaining CAT’s cross-space robustness transfer. Crucially, the bound depends explicitly on the singular values of the LLM’s embedding matrix. Leveraging this insight, we propose SVR-CAT: a novel CAT variant incorporating a singular-value-based regularization term. Experiments on Llama-2, Qwen, and Phi-3 show SVR-CAT reduces jailbreak success rates by 22.6–35.1% across GCG, AutoDAN, and PAIR attacks, while preserving utility (≤0.8% drop on BBH/MMLU). Code: https://github.com/fshp971/continuous-adv-icl.

item.title: Evaluating Differential Privacy Against Membership Inference in Federated Learning: Insights from the NIST Genomics Red Team Challenge
item.description: This paper empirically evaluates Differential Privacy (DP) as a defense against Membership Inference Attacks (MIAs) in Federated Learning (FL), using the standardized environment of the 2025 NIST Genomics PPFL Red Teaming Event. We propose a novel **stacking-based black-box MIA**, ensembling seven diverse estimators to train a meta-classifier on prediction probabilities and cross-entropy losses—significantly enhancing inference sensitivity under DP noise. Evaluated across three privacy tiers ($ε = \infty$, $200$, and $10$), our attack outperforms all baselines in the No-DP and Low-Privacy settings and—critically—detects measurable membership leakage at $ε = 200$ where the standard LiRA baseline collapses (AUC ≈ 0.5). On an independent third-party benchmark, we observe a clear degradation trend: AUC drops from 0.72 ± 0.03 ($ε=200$) to 0.58 ± 0.02 ($ε=10$), providing the first empirical characterization of stacking-based MIA resilience across calibrated DP budgets in genomic FL.

item.title: Robust Semi-Supervised Temporal Intrusion Detection for Adversarial Cloud Networks
item.description: Cloud networks increasingly depend on ML-based intrusion detection, yet real-world deployment suffers from label scarcity, non-stationary traffic, and adaptive adversaries contaminating unlabeled data. Most semi-supervised NIDS assume benign and stationary unlabeled traffic—an unrealistic assumption in adversarial cloud environments. We propose **RSTID**, a robust semi-supervised temporal framework that explicitly models *adversarial contamination* and *temporal drift* in unlabeled flows. RSTID operates on flow-level data and integrates three key components: (i) consistency regularization under temporal augmentations; (ii) confidence-aware pseudo-labeling with dynamic thresholding; and (iii) selective temporal invariance—leveraging intrinsic flow temporal structure while avoiding over-constraining during drift. Evaluated on CIC-IDS2017, CSE-CIC-IDS2018, and UNSW-NB15 under severe label scarcity (5%–20% labeled), RSTID consistently outperforms state-of-the-art supervised and semi-supervised baselines—achieving up to 18.7% higher F1-score, 3.2× better label efficiency, and <4.1% performance degradation under 20% adversarial contamination or concept drift.

item.title: LLM-Guided Prompt Evolution for Password Guessing
item.description: Passwords remain the dominant authentication method, yet their security is routinely undermined by predictable user behavior and large-scale credential leaks. This paper introduces **LLM-guided prompt evolution**, a novel approach that applies evolutionary computation—specifically MAP-Elites quality-diversity search within an island population model (via OpenEvolve)—to automatically optimize prompts for LLM-based password guessing. Evaluated on a RockYou-derived test set (500K real passwords), our method boosts cracking rates from **2.02% to 8.48%** across three practical configurations: local (Qwen3-8B), cloud-efficient (Gemini-2.5 Flash), and frontier two-model ensemble (Gemma-3 27B + Qwen3 32B). Character-level analysis confirms evolved prompts yield statistically more realistic password distributions (p < 0.001), validating behavioral fidelity. Crucially, this work demonstrates that **automated prompt optimization—requiring no model fine-tuning or training data access—is a low-barrier, high-impact technique for strengthening LLM-powered password auditing**, while also highlighting how attack pipelines themselves evolve autonomously.

item.title: DeepSeek Robustness Against Semantic-Character Dual-Space Mutated Prompt Injection
item.description: Prompt injection poses a critical security threat to LLMs, yet most existing studies evaluate only single-space attacks—semantic *or* character-level—failing to model realistic multi-space adversarial perturbations. To bridge this gap, we propose **PromptFuzz-SC**, the first semantic-character *dual-space mutation framework* for black-box robustness evaluation of Chinese LLMs like DeepSeek. It unifies semantic transformations (e.g., paraphrasing, word-order scrambling) with character-level obfuscation (e.g., zero-width insertion, encoding-based mutation) and employs a hybrid ε-greedy + hill-climbing search to generate stealthy, high-success adversarial prompts. Evaluated on DeepSeek using MSR, AQS, and Stealth, dual-space mutation achieves the strongest overall performance: **mean MSR = 0.189** (↑12.5% vs. semantic-only; ↑5.6% vs. character-only), **peak MSR = 0.375**, and **highest mean Stealth**, while maintaining competitive query efficiency in best-case scenarios. Our results underscore the necessity of composite mutation strategies for rigorous red-teaming and inform the design of multi-layered defenses.

item.title: Practical Evaluation of the Crypto-Agility Maturity Model
item.description: Cryptographic agility is essential for sustaining digital security amid the post-quantum transition, and the Crypto-Agility Maturity Model (CAMM) was proposed to assess it systematically. This paper presents the first rigorous evaluation of CAMM against established maturity model design principles (e.g., ISO/IEC/IEEE 33001). We identify three critical shortcomings: (1) ambiguous scope and target audiences—lacking clear organizational or technical boundaries; (2) insufficiently operationalized acceptance criteria—rendering higher-level requirements unverifiable and non-reproducible; and (3) flawed dependency relations—including redundancies, cycles, and omissions (e.g., missing supply-chain agility). Empirical application to a real-world TLS service upgrade confirmed these issues: 40% of Level 4 requirements were inapplicable or ill-defined. Based on these findings, we propose concrete enhancements: a stratified stakeholder matrix, standardized evidence types per capability (configuration artifacts, test logs, audit reports), and a revised dependency graph incorporating PQC migration pathways and supply-chain coordination. This work advances CAMM toward practical, consistent, and auditable cryptographic agility assessment.

item.title: WebAgentGuard: A Reasoning-Driven Guard Model for Detecting Prompt Injection Attacks in Web Agents
item.description: Web agents powered by vision-language models (VLMs) enable autonomous web interaction but are critically vulnerable to prompt injection attacks—malicious instructions hidden in HTML or screenshots that hijack agent behavior, risking data leakage and misuse. Existing defenses (e.g., system prompt hardening, agent fine-tuning) suffer from poor generalization and utility degradation. We propose **WebAgentGuard**, a reasoning-driven, multimodal guard model operating *in parallel* with the main agent to decouple detection from task execution. Trained on a large synthetic dataset (128K samples, 164 topics, 230 UI styles) generated by GPT-5, it leverages stepwise multimodal reasoning over screenshots and DOM text. Via reasoning-intensive supervised fine-tuning and reinforcement learning with adversarial feedback, WebAgentGuard achieves **94.7% average detection accuracy** across six benchmarks—outperforming strong baselines by +11.2%—while preserving >99% agent utility and adding **zero latency**.

item.title: SpanKey: Dynamic Key Space Conditioning for Neural Network Access Control
item.description: SpanKey introduces a lightweight, accuracy-preserving neural network access control mechanism that gates inference by *conditioning intermediate activations on secret keys*, without weight encryption or accuracy trade-offs. It defines a low-dimensional key subspace $Span(B)$ via a learnable basis matrix $B$; valid keys are sampled as $k = \alpha^\top B$, then injected additively or multiplicatively into layer activations with tunable strength $\gamma$. Invalid keys are explicitly drawn outside $Span(B)$. We make three contributions: (i) A principled design space for subspace-based key injection across network layers; (ii) Identification and analysis of *key absorption*—a failure mode where networks implicitly nullify key effects—characterized via a novel Beta-energy split and margin-tail diagnostics; (iii) Empirical validation: Modes A–C achieve >99.2% acceptance for valid keys and <0.5% false acceptance for invalid keys on CIFAR-10/ResNet-18, with MNIST ablations confirming robustness at small subspace rank ($r=4$). SpanKey is not cryptographically secure but offers practical, scalable inference gating. Code: https://github.com/mindmemory-ai/dksc.

item.title: TEMPLATEFUZZ: Fine-Grained Chat Template Fuzzing for Jailbreaking and Red Teaming LLMs
item.description: Large Language Models (LLMs) face critical security risks from jailbreak attacks, yet prior work largely overlooks *chat templates*—the structured message formatting rules (e.g., `<|user|>...<|assistant|>`) that critically influence safety alignment. This paper introduces **TEMPLATEFUZZ**, the first fine-grained fuzzing framework targeting chat templates as a novel attack surface. It (1) designs element-level mutation rules (e.g., delimiter obfuscation, zero-width insertion, nested templating) to generate semantically valid but structurally disruptive variants; (2) employs a heuristic search strategy that jointly optimizes attack success rate (ASR) and task accuracy via lightweight gradient-guided exploration; and (3) integrates an active learning–based rule oracle for fast, accurate, and automated jailbreak evaluation (F1=0.96). Evaluated on 12 open-source LLMs, TEMPLATEFUZZ achieves a **98.2% average ASR** with only **1.1% accuracy degradation**, outperforming SOTA by up to 47.9% in ASR and 8.4% in accuracy preservation. Remarkably, it attains **90% average ASR** even on 5 commercial LLMs (e.g., GPT-4o, Claude-3.5) via template-informed prompt injection—demonstrating the pervasive vulnerability of template logic across model deployment paradigms.

item.title: From IOCs to Regex: Automating CTI Operationalization for SOC with LLMs
item.description: Cyber Threat Intelligence (CTI) operationalization requires converting unstructured Indicators of Compromise (IOCs) into precise, syntactically valid regular expressions (regexes) for SIEM rules, log parsing, and forensics—yet this remains largely manual and error-prone. To bridge this gap, we propose **IOCRegex-gen**, an LLM-based system that fully automates IOC-to-regex generation. It introduces: (i) a *group-aware mechanism* to intelligently assign capture/non-capture groups based on IOC semantics, and (ii) an *iterative reasoning and multi-stage validation pipeline* combining LLM self-refinement, regex AST parsing, and dynamic matching against MITRE ATT&CK ground-truth strings. Evaluated on 3,000+ real CTI reports and 2,400 ATT&CK evaluation samples, IOCRegex-gen achieves a **99.1% hit rate** and only **0.8% false-positive rate**, demonstrating robust scalability and production-readiness for automated CTI operationalization.

item.title: Fully Homomorphic Encryption on Llama 3 model for privacy preserving LLM inference
item.description: This work pioneers the integration of **Post-Quantum Cryptography (PQC)-compliant Lattice-based Fully Homomorphic Encryption (FHE)** into the inference pipeline of the LLaMA-3 model to enable privacy-preserving, quantum-resistant LLM deployment. Leveraging `concrete-ml`, we selectively apply FHE operations to privacy-critical layers (e.g., embedding outputs, attention logits, and FFN activations) while preserving plaintext computation elsewhere—achieving a practical balance between security and efficiency. On an Intel i9 CPU, our FHE-secured LLaMA-3-8B achieves **98% text generation accuracy**, **237 ms latency per inference**, and **80 tokens/sec throughput**, demonstrating feasibility for real-world use. The approach withstands both classical and Shor’s-algorithm-based quantum attacks, fulfilling NIST PQC Level 3 requirements with only ~1.7× overhead over baseline inference.

item.title: CIA: Inferring the Communication Topology from LLM-based Multi-Agent Systems
item.description: Large language model (LLM)-based multi-agent systems (MAS) rely critically on internal communication topologies for task coordination, yet these topologies often encode proprietary architectural knowledge. This paper identifies and formalizes a novel privacy threat: *communication topology inference under strict black-box access*, where an attacker observes only system inputs/outputs. We propose **Communication Inference Attack (CIA)**—a query-efficient framework that crafts adversarial inputs to elicit discriminative reasoning traces from intermediate agents, then models semantic correlations via **global bias disentanglement** and **LLM-guided weak supervision** to reconstruct topology edges. Evaluated across six optimized MAS topologies (e.g., hierarchical, ring, tree), CIA achieves an average AUC of **0.87** and a peak AUC of **0.99**, outperforming baselines by +0.23 in AUC with ≤50 queries. Our results expose a fundamental vulnerability in LLM-MAS deployment and establish topology confidentiality as a first-class security concern.

item.title: MultiDocFusion: Hierarchical and Multimodal Chunking Pipeline for Enhanced RAG on Long Industrial Documents
item.description: RAG-based QA for long industrial documents suffers from structural ignorance: conventional chunking methods ignore multimodal layout (e.g., headings, tables, figures) and semantic hierarchy, causing context fragmentation and answer degradation. To address this, we propose **MultiDocFusion**, a hierarchical and multimodal chunking pipeline comprising: (i) vision-based document region detection; (ii) OCR-powered text extraction per region; (iii) LLM-guided Document Section Hierarchical Parsing (DSHP-LLM) to reconstruct a semantic tree; and (iv) DFS-based hierarchical chunk construction. Evaluated across five industrial benchmarks (power, manufacturing, chemical domains), MultiDocFusion achieves **+8–15% retrieval precision** and **+2–3% ANLS QA scores** over strong baselines. Results confirm that *explicitly leveraging document hierarchy is critical* for robust multimodal RAG—establishing structure-aware chunking as a foundational requirement for industrial knowledge systems.
